cards.name: Five of Wands
cards.progression_meaning: conflict / competition / creative friction — Fire's expansion meets opposing fires; the five wands clash in mid-air but no one is wounded, the friction itself is the work
cards.progression_position: 5 = the conflict/challenge node of Fire's progression: the celebration of the Four has dissolved into rivalry — five youths swing wands in a free-for-all, each trying to land his version of the vision; competition that is generative rather than destructive if it can be channelled
upright_keywords: the brainstorm that becomes a battle
cards.upright_meaning: Five youths clash with raised wands in a barren field, each aiming from a different angle — no blood, no fallen bodies, but a real tangle of competing energies. The Five of Wands is Fire's friction: the moment passionate, divergent visions meet and none is willing to yield. Upright, this card does not curse; it names the genuine productive tension of competition — the brainstorm that has become a battle, the team of strong voices that has not yet found its shared song. The work is to let the friction refine rather than fragment.
cards.reversed_meaning: Reversed, the same friction either resolves into alignment — the disagreement that finally finds its common ground — or curdles into avoidance, where the conflict is suppressed rather than worked through and the smoke collects under the door. The shadow is not the conflict itself; it is the refusal to let it surface honestly. The card invites you to ask which disagreement you have been smoothing over that needs to be openly engaged for the team (or the inner self) to find its real alignment.
cards.psychological_lens: constructive conflict + diversity of perspective; the difference between generative friction that sharpens thought and disintegrative rivalry that exhausts it
cards.eastern_anchor: The Five of Wands resonates with the East Asian martial image of '切磋' (qiēcuō — to hone one's skill through friendly but real contest with peers) and the Buddhist observation that '逆增上缘' (adverse conditions can become the cause of growth). Tiger's Eye, traditionally the stone of the warrior who fights with honour and protects what matters, pairs with this Fire friction that can sharpen rather than wound.
cards.recommended_practice: Hold a tiger-eye stone; name one conflict you have been avoiding. Ask whether engaging it honestly — for the work, not the ego — would sharpen or fragment the situation.
cards.love_meaning: In love readings, the Five of Wands signals genuine friction between partners — bickering, competing visions, the spark that has become a contest. The card does not predict the relationship's failure; it asks whether the friction is sharpening the bond or wearing it down.
cards.career_meaning: In career, this card points to competition, rival viewpoints, a brainstorm that has turned into a battle. Real tension; the work is to channel it toward a better outcome than any single view could reach.
cards.finances_meaning: For finances, the Five of Wands can signal competing financial priorities or a bidding/contest situation. Strategic framing of competing claims, not specific investment advice.
cards.love_meaning_reversed: Reversed in love: the conflict resolving, or — shadow — being suppressed rather than worked through, the resentment gathering underground.
cards.career_meaning_reversed: Reversed in career: the team finding alignment, or — shadow — the disagreement silenced rather than resolved, harmony performed.
cards.finances_meaning_reversed: Reversed in finances: competing claims finding order, or avoidance of an honest financial conversation. (Not investment advice.)

best_overall.reason: Tiger's Eye (Fire, Courage / Protection / Clarity of intent) is the honourable-conflict stone — the courage to engage friction honestly and the clarity to channel it toward refinement rather than fragmentation.

best_upright.name: Carnelian
best_upright.reason: Carnelian (Fire, Courage / Motivation) keeps the contest energised without tipping into hostility.

best_reversed.name: Rose Quartz
best_reversed.reason: When friction needs to resolve into alignment, Rose Quartz (Heart) softens the contest back toward the shared purpose that made it worth fighting over.

cards.name: Six of Wands
cards.progression_meaning: victory / public recognition / the harvest of Fire's contest — the figure rides back through the crowd, laurel-wreathed, the friction of the Five resolved into a recognised win
cards.progression_position: 6 = the harmony/victory node of Fire's progression: the conflict of the Five has been channelled into a clear outcome — the rider on the laurel-wreathed horse, the crowd around him raising wands in salute, the public moment where effort meets recognition
reversed_keywords: the win that does not land
cards.upright_meaning: A rider on a white horse moves through a crowd, a wreath of laurel on his brow and a wand wreathed in the same victory leaves raised upright in his hand; the crowd around him raises wands in salute. The Six of Wands is Fire's public victory: the friction of the Five has been resolved, the contest won, the effort recognised by the community it was offered to. Upright, this card honours genuine, earned recognition — the moment of being seen for work that was actually done, leadership validated by those it served.
cards.reversed_meaning: Reversed, the same victory wobbles — recognition delayed, the win that does not quite land, ego outpacing substance, or a public moment that masks a fragile foundation beneath. The shadow is not the recognition itself; it is the inflation that hides what was never really built. The card invites you to ask whether the crown you are wearing rests on work that can actually carry its weight, and whose voices besides your own are confirming it.
cards.psychological_lens: external recognition + the difference between earned confidence and narcissistic inflation; the role of legitimate validation in sustaining long-term effort
cards.eastern_anchor: The Six of Wands resonates with the East Asian image of '衣锦还乡' (returning home in brocade, the honoured return of one whose effort the community has recognised) and the warrior's dignified homecoming. Sunstone, with its sun-sparkle optical effect traditionally associated with radiance and visible triumph, pairs with this crowned, publicly-seen victory.
cards.recommended_practice: Hold a sunstone at the solar plexus; name one recognition you have recently received. Receive it without deflection for one full breath — then name one person whose contribution made it possible.
cards.love_meaning: In love readings, the Six of Wands signals being seen and chosen — a relationship (or potential partner) that openly honours you, not one that hides the connection. Recognition that is mutual, not performed.
cards.career_meaning: In career, this card is one of the strongest recognition omens — promotion, public praise, a launched project that lands, leadership validated by those it was built for. Earned, not inflated.
cards.finances_meaning: For finances, the Six of Wands supports financial recognition — a raise, a paid invoice, a venture that returns visibly. Pair with grounded stewardship so the win becomes foundation rather than spectacle.
cards.love_meaning_reversed: Reversed in love: recognition withheld, a relationship hidden, or a partner whose affection is more performance than substance.
cards.career_meaning_reversed: Reversed in career: recognition delayed, ego outpacing the work, or a public win masking a fragile foundation.
cards.finances_meaning_reversed: Reversed in finances: the win that does not quite land, recognition without the substance to sustain it. (Not investment advice.)

best_upright.name: Citrine
best_upright.reason: Citrine (Fire, Abundance / Confidence) amplifies the abundance of a victory genuinely earned.

best_reversed.name: Tiger's Eye
best_reversed.reason: When victory wobbles into inflation, Tiger's Eye (Clarity / Protection) returns the gaze to the real foundation beneath the crown.

best_daily_wear.name: Carnelian

cards.name: Seven of Wands
cards.progression_meaning: defend position / hold the high ground / the challenge after the victory — Fire's recognised win immediately challenged, the figure on the height fending off rivals below
cards.progression_position: 7 = the assessment/defense node of Fire's progression: the victory of the Six has made the position worth taking — the figure stands on the high ground, six wands raised against him from below, his own wand the seventh held to defend what he has built
cards.upright_meaning: A figure stands on raised ground, six wands jabbing up at him from below; he holds his own wand — the seventh — diagonally to fend them off, his posture defiant, his footing higher than the challenge. The Seven of Wands is Fire's defensive stand: the victory of the Six has made the position worth contesting, and now the work is to hold what was won. Upright, this card honours the courage of protecting what matters — a boundary, a vision, an achievement — against real pressure from those who would erode or appropriate it.
cards.reversed_meaning: Reversed, the same defense either falters — exhaustion, the line not held, ground yielded under sustained pressure — or marks the moment to ask whether this particular fight is still worth fighting. The shadow is not the defense itself; it is the confusion of stubbornness with principle, holding a position out of pride that no longer serves the vision. The card invites you to ask which ground you are defending because it is truly yours to hold, and which you are defending only because retreating would feel like losing.
cards.psychological_lens: boundary maintenance + the cost of sustained vigilance; the difference between principled defense and defended ego
cards.eastern_anchor: The Seven of Wands resonates with the East Asian martial image of '守山' (holding the high ground) and the warrior's discernment of which fights are worth the cost of standing. Tiger's Eye, the stone of the warrior who protects with clear intent, pairs with this defiant stand on the height.
cards.recommended_practice: Hold a tiger-eye stone; name one position you are currently defending. Ask whether it is truly yours to hold, or whether yielding it would free fire for a worthier field.
cards.love_meaning: In love readings, the Seven of Wands signals a relationship (or boundary within one) being defended against external pressure — family, rivals, competing demands. The card asks whether the defense is principled or merely stubborn.
cards.career_meaning: In career, this card points to defending a position, a project, or a leadership role against real challenge. Hold the high ground where it is principled; release where it is only pride.
cards.finances_meaning: For finances, the Seven of Wands can signal defending a financial position or boundary — protecting what has been built against erosion. Strategic framing, not specific investment advice.
cards.love_meaning_reversed: Reversed in love: the boundary not held, exhaustion in defending the relationship, or stubbornness mistaken for principle.
cards.career_meaning_reversed: Reversed in career: overwhelmed, ground yielded, or fighting a battle that no longer serves the work.
cards.finances_meaning_reversed: Reversed in finances: the position eroded, or defending financial ground that would be wiser to cede. (Not investment advice.)

best_overall.name: Tiger's Eye
best_overall.reason: Tiger's Eye (Fire, Courage / Protection / Clarity) is the defensive-stand stone — the courage to hold the high ground with clear discernment of which fights are truly worth the cost.

best_upright.name: Carnelian
best_upright.reason: Carnelian (Fire, Courage / Motivation) sustains the defiant energy required to hold the line under pressure.

best_reversed.name: Hematite
best_reversed.reason: When defense falters into exhaustion, Hematite (Grounding / Strength / Endurance) returns stamina to the body and roots the stand back into the earth.

best_love.name: Rose Quartz
best_love.reason: Cross-card unified love intention; boundaries held with warmth.

best_daily_wear.name: Sunstone
best_daily_wear.reason: Sunstone worn daily keeps confidence and morale steady during sustained defensive periods.

cards.related_reason: Five→Six→Seven→Nine progression in Wands (friction → victory → defend → resilience); Nine of Wands is the sustained-endurance companion; Strength (Major 8) shares the courage-and-endurance theme.
cards.source_notes: Biddy/Labyrinthos do not list crystals. Tiger's Eye chosen best_overall per fire-suit consensus (Seven of Wands = defensive stand, Tiger's Eye = protector stone of clear-intent defense). Research §3.3 Wands 7 = '捍卫立场 / 坚守'.

cards.name: Eight of Wands
cards.progression_meaning: velocity / rapid movement / momentum unblocked — Fire's energy released after the stand of the Seven, eight wands streaking across open sky toward their target
cards.progression_position: 8 = the mastery/velocity node of Fire's progression: the defensive stand of the Seven has cleared the path, and now eight wands fly across a clear sky — pure directed momentum, the logjam broken, the message finally arriving
cards.upright_meaning: Eight wands streak across a clear blue sky in a diagonal line, like arrows in flight or spears thrown — the only Minor card with no human figure, because the subject is the motion itself. The Eight of Wands is Fire's pure velocity: the stand of the Seven has cleared the path, and now energy that has been held back is suddenly, decisively released. Upright, this card is the breakthrough of momentum — news arriving, a project suddenly unblocked, events accelerating after a long stall, the green light that finally comes.
cards.reversed_meaning: Reversed, the same velocity meets friction — delays, resistance, momentum scattering into too many directions, or the message that does not arrive. The shadow is not the slowness itself; it is the forcing of speed against a door that is not yet ready to open. The card invites you to ask whether the resistance is something to push through or something asking for a different timing, and to let the wands re-align before they are released.
cards.psychological_lens: momentum + the threshold between productive acceleration and reactive forcing; the difference between aligned movement and scattered speed
cards.eastern_anchor: The Eight of Wands resonates with the East Asian martial image of '箭在弦上' (the arrow nocked and released, the moment of irreversible flight) and the Zen archer's clarity that the arrow in flight cannot be hurried or recalled. Carnelian, traditionally a stone of activated life-force and swift courage, pairs with this Fire released into pure momentum.
cards.recommended_practice: Hold a carnelian in your dominant hand; name one stalled situation that just received its green light. Identify the single next action that the momentum is asking for — and take it before the window closes.
cards.love_meaning: In love readings, the Eight of Wands signals rapid movement — a connection accelerating fast, a long-awaited message arriving, a relationship suddenly shifting gear after a stall. Real momentum; ensure the direction is still the one you want.
cards.career_meaning: In career, this card signals events suddenly unblocked — a project greenlit, news arriving, momentum returning after a long stall. Excellent for launches and time-sensitive action.
cards.finances_meaning: For finances, the Eight of Wands can signal delayed money finally arriving, or a financial process suddenly accelerating. Strategic framing, not specific investment advice.
cards.love_meaning_reversed: Reversed in love: momentum stalling, the message delayed, a connection losing pace just when it seemed ready to move.
cards.career_meaning_reversed: Reversed in career: delays returning, friction against momentum, or speed forced against a door not ready to open.
cards.finances_meaning_reversed: Reversed in finances: the delay continuing, or momentum scattering into too many directions. (Not investment advice.)

cards.name: Nine of Wands
cards.progression_meaning: resilience / final endurance / the last stand before completion — Fire's near-completion node, the weary sentinel who has held the line through eight battles and braces for the ninth
cards.progression_position: 9 = the near-completion / resilience node of Fire's progression: the velocity of the Eight has spent the body, and now the figure stands wounded but upright, leaning on one wand with eight more lined behind him — the battle-tested sentinel holding the final perimeter before the tenth brings the cycle home
reversed_keywords: fighting on when the war is over
cards.upright_meaning: A sentinel with a bandaged head leans on a single wand; behind him a barrier of eight more wands is lined upright, and his posture is weary but unyielding — he has been through eight battles and is bracing for the ninth. The Nine of Wands is Fire's resilience at its near-limit: the body spent, the test of stamina still one more round to go, the determination to hold the perimeter until the cycle completes. Upright, this card honours the dignity of sustained endurance — the part of you that has been tested by real adversity and is still standing, asking only for the stamina to finish what was started.
cards.reversed_meaning: Reversed, the same endurance either falters into exhaustion — burnout, the body refusing another round — or curdles into defended paranoia, fighting battles in a war that has already ended because standing down feels more dangerous than the vigil. The shadow is not the resilience itself; it is the refusal to recognise when the perimeter is no longer needed. The card invites you to ask which battle you are still fighting that has actually already been won, and what rest your body has been refusing that the completion of the cycle requires.
cards.psychological_lens: grit + post-fatigue vigilance; the difference between resilient persistence and trauma-driven hyper-vigilance that cannot stand down
cards.eastern_anchor: The Nine of Wands resonates with the East Asian virtue of '坚忍' (jiānrěn — unyielding endurance) and the martial image of the sentinel who holds the wall through the night. Red Jasper, traditionally a stone of steady stamina and the endurance of the long march, pairs with this battle-tested resilience.
cards.recommended_practice: Hold a red-jasper at the root; name one battle you have been fighting past the point where it was actually won. Allow yourself one real breath of standing down before the next decision.
cards.love_meaning: In love readings, the Nine of Wands signals a relationship that has been tested by adversity and is still standing — weary but committed, the final stretch of a hard season. The card asks whether the vigil is still serving the bond or has become the bond's armour.
cards.career_meaning: In career, this card points to the final endurance stretch — a project or role reaching completion through sustained stamina. Real grit; pair with honest rest so the finish is reached intact.
cards.finances_meaning: For finances, the Nine of Wands can signal financial resilience tested and held — sustaining through a difficult stretch toward stability. Strategic framing, not specific investment advice.
cards.love_meaning_reversed: Reversed in love: exhaustion in the relationship, fighting battles that are already over, or vigilance that has hardened into defensive distance.
cards.career_meaning_reversed: Reversed in career: burnout, the body refusing another round, or paranoia defending a perimeter no longer under threat.
cards.finances_meaning_reversed: Reversed in finances: depletion, or financial vigilance that has curdled into fear-based hoarding. (Not investment advice.)

cards.name: Three of Cups
cards.suit_theme: emotion / intuition / relationship / creativity of the heart
cards.progression_meaning: celebration / friendship / creative expression in community — the moment Water's union of two expands into the joy of three, the first shared harvest of the heart
cards.progression_position: 3 = the early-expansion node of Water's progression: where the Two of Cups joined two hearts, the Three of Cups expands the union into a circle — three dancers raising their cups together, the bounty of friendship, the first creative expression offered to a community
reversed_keywords: feeling on the outside of the circle
cards.upright_meaning: Three women in floral garlands dance together in a field, each raising a cup; behind them a harvest of fruit and pumpkins lies on the ground — the picture of shared joy, friendship celebrated, the bounty of the heart offered to a circle rather than held by a pair. The Three of Cups is Water's expansion beyond the dyad: the union of the Two has become the gathering of three, the first creative expression in community. Upright, this card honours the genuine nourishment of friendship — the friends who see you, the shared joy that multiplies rather than divides, the creative collaboration that none of you could have built alone.
cards.reversed_meaning: Reversed, the same gathering meets friction — overindulgence that crosses the line from joy to excess, a third party disrupting a bond, gossip corroding the circle, or the quiet ache of feeling on the outside of a celebration you were not invited into. The shadow is not the friendship itself; it is the imbalance hidden inside the gathering. The card invites you to ask which of your circles has become a place you perform rather than belong, and what it would take to return to genuine exchange.
cards.psychological_lens: communal belonging + creative collaboration; the difference between nourishing friendship and social performance that drains
cards.eastern_anchor: The Three of Cups resonates with the East Asian image of '桃园三结义' (the peach-garden oath of three brothers) and the festival circle of friends whose bond is honoured across the seasons. Aquamarine, traditionally the stone of honest, flowing communication between people, pairs with this circle of cups raised together.
cards.recommended_practice: Hold an aquamarine at the throat; name one friendship circle that has nourished you recently. Speak one honest appreciation to one member of it this week.
cards.love_meaning: In love readings, the Three of Cups signals the role of friendship and community in a relationship — shared friends, a celebration (engagement, wedding), or the reminder that a healthy pair is nourished by a healthy circle around it.
cards.career_meaning: In career, this card favours creative collaboration, team celebration, networks of genuine professional friendship, and any work whose output is enriched by a diverse circle.
cards.finances_meaning: For finances, the Three of Cups can signal shared abundance — joint ventures among friends, collaborative income, the wealth of networks. Pair with clear terms so the friendship is protected.
cards.love_meaning_reversed: Reversed in love: third-party interference, gossip, or a relationship that has become more performance than genuine bond.
cards.career_meaning_reversed: Reversed in career: office gossip, a team fragmented, or celebration that masks unresolved conflict.
cards.finances_meaning_reversed: Reversed in finances: shared ventures turning sour, unclear terms corroding friendship, over-spending in social settings.

cards.name: Four of Cups
cards.suit_theme: emotion / intuition / relationship / creativity of the heart
cards.progression_meaning: apathy / discontent / refusing the new offering — Water's stillness curdling into indifference, three cups offered and a fourth refused by a figure bored with what is being given
cards.progression_position: 4 = the stabilization/stillness node of Water's progression turned apathetic: where the other suits' Four stabilises, Cups' Four is the famous dissatisfaction — the figure sitting under the tree with three cups before him, arms crossed, refusing the fourth cup offered by a cloud because what is being given does not match what he wants
cards.upright_meaning: A youth sits beneath a tree, arms crossed, three cups arrayed before him on the grass; from a cloud a hand offers a fourth cup, and the figure's gaze is fixed elsewhere — bored, dissatisfied, unwilling to receive what is being given because it does not match the want inside. The Four of Cups is Water turned apathetic: the moment the heart, offered a real gift, refuses it out of discontent or inattention. Upright, this card does not shame the flatness; it names the very human experience of emotional staleness and gently observes that something is being offered that the closed posture cannot receive.
cards.reversed_meaning: Reversed, the same apathy begins to lift — acceptance returning, the offered cup finally received, alertness to what was being missed, gratitude dawning after a period of flatness. The reversal marks the willingness to uncross the arms and let the offering in. This card invites you to ask what has been offered to you recently that your discontent has been refusing, and whether the want inside you is asking to be re-examined rather than fed.
cards.psychological_lens: emotional habituation + the negativity bias that discounts available gifts; the difference between genuine discernment and chronic dissatisfaction
cards.eastern_anchor: The Four of Cups resonates with the Chan Buddhist observation that '平常心是道' (the ordinary mind is the path) — and the suffering that comes from missing the offering already present while chasing the one imagined. Moonstone, traditionally a stone of receptive inner seeing, pairs with this card's invitation to re-examine what is being refused.
cards.recommended_practice: Hold a moonstone at the heart; name one offering you have recently been too flat or discontented to receive. Uncross your arms for one full breath and let yourself consider it.
cards.love_meaning: In love readings, the Four of Cups signals emotional flatness or discontent in a relationship — boredom, taking the partner for granted, or refusing an offering of connection because it does not match an imagined ideal. The card asks whether the want needs re-examining.
cards.career_meaning: In career, this card points to apathy or missed opportunity — a role, an offer, a creative possibility being refused out of dissatisfaction rather than discernment.
cards.finances_meaning: For finances, the Four of Cups can signal financial discontent — chasing an imagined gain while missing the offering already available. Strategic framing, not specific investment advice.
cards.love_meaning_reversed: Reversed in love: the offered cup received, gratitude returning, the relationship re-engaged after a period of flatness.
cards.career_meaning_reversed: Reversed in career: alertness to opportunity returning, the gift finally accepted, apathy breaking.
cards.finances_meaning_reversed: Reversed in finances: a missed financial opportunity finally recognised and received. (Not investment advice.)

best_overall.reason: Moonstone (Water, Intuition / New Beginnings) is the apathy-lifting stone — the receptive inner seeing that re-opens the heart to the offering it has been refusing.

best_upright.reason: Citrine (Fire-but-sunny, Joy / Abundance) gently warms the emotional flatness back toward engagement.

cards.name: Five of Cups
cards.suit_theme: emotion / intuition / relationship / creativity of the heart
cards.progression_meaning: loss / regret / focus on what is spilled — Water's conflict node, the figure in black cloak mourning three overturned cups while two full ones stand behind him unattended
cards.progression_position: 5 = the conflict/loss node of Water's progression: the apathy of the Four has deepened into genuine grief — three cups overturned and spilled, a bridge over a stream crossed only by the figure's refusal to turn around, and behind him two cups still full and unattended
cards.upright_meaning: A figure in a black cloak stands beside three overturned cups, golden liquid spilled on the ground; a bridge crosses the stream behind him toward what looks like home, and behind the figure two cups stand full and upright — unattended, because his gaze is fixed on what is lost. The Five of Cups is Water's grief: the moment the heart turns toward what cannot be recovered and away from what remains. Upright, this card honours genuine loss without medicalising it — a real grief, a real regret, a wound that deserves to be felt — and gently observes that two cups are still standing.
cards.reversed_meaning: Reversed, the same grief begins to integrate — acceptance arriving, the figure slowly turning toward the two full cups still standing, forgiveness (of self or other) becoming possible, the willingness to cross the bridge toward what remains rather than stand at the spilled ones forever. The reversal does not erase the loss; it marks the heart's slow willingness to receive what is still here. This card invites you to ask what grief has been ready to soften, and what full cup you have not yet let yourself turn toward.
cards.psychological_lens: loss processing + attentional bias toward the negative; the documented role of grief work and the slow re-orientation toward remaining goods
cards.eastern_anchor: The Five of Cups resonates with the East Asian image of '覆水难收' (spilled water cannot be gathered) and the Buddhist practice of mourning what is gone without abandoning what remains. Rose Quartz, the house stone of the heart across both Western and Eastern traditions, pairs with this card's call to hold the grieving heart gently while turning, slowly, toward the cups still standing.
cards.recommended_practice: Hold a rose-quartz at the heart; name one loss you have been fixed on. Allow one breath of grief for it — then name one cup still standing behind you that you have not yet turned toward.
cards.love_meaning: In love readings, the Five of Cups names a real loss within or around a relationship — a breakup, a disappointment, a regret deserving to be felt honestly. The card does not curse the future; it asks whether the gaze fixed on the spilled cups is keeping you from the ones still standing.
cards.career_meaning: In career, this card can signal a real disappointment — a lost role, a project that did not work, a regret to be felt. Allow the grief; then assess what remains that deserves attention.
cards.finances_meaning: For finances, the Five of Cups can name a financial loss or regret that needs acknowledging before it can be addressed. This is not investment advice; it observes the experience of loss and invites attention to what remains workable.
cards.love_meaning_reversed: Reversed in love: grief integrating, forgiveness beginning, the heart turning toward a relationship (or a new one) still standing.
cards.career_meaning_reversed: Reversed in career: the disappointment integrating, attention returning to what remains and what is still possible.
cards.finances_meaning_reversed: Reversed in finances: the loss acknowledged, attention returning to what is still workable. (Not investment advice.)

best_overall.name: Rose Quartz
best_overall.reason: Rose Quartz (Heart, unconditional love) is the grieving-heart stone — the gentle holding that lets a real loss be felt without abandoning the cups still standing.

best_upright.name: Rhodochrosite
best_upright.reason: Rhodochrosite (Heart, self-love / inner-child healing) is the classic stone for tending the tender place where regret has gathered.

best_reversed.reason: When grief begins to integrate, Moonstone (Water, New Beginnings) supports the slow turn toward what remains and the willingness to receive it.

best_daily_wear.reason: Aquamarine worn daily supports calm, honest expression of the grief so it does not calcify into silence.

cards.name: Six of Cups
cards.suit_theme: emotion / intuition / relationship / creativity of the heart
cards.progression_meaning: nostalgia / innocence / childhood remembering — Water's harmony node turned backward, the figure receiving a cup brimming with flowers from a smaller child in a sunlit town of the past
cards.progression_position: 6 = the harmony/nostalgia node of Water's progression: the grief of the Five has softened into tender remembering — an older figure accepts a cup of flowers from a younger one, six cups filled with blooms arranged around them, a sunlit town of childhood behind them, the harmony of innocence remembered
cards.upright_meaning: In a sunlit town of flowered cups, an older figure in a white tunic accepts a cup brimming with blossoms from a smaller child; six cups filled with flowers are arranged around them, and the air is one of gentle, generous remembering. The Six of Cups is Water turned tender toward the past: the moment nostalgia softens rather than grieves, the inner child met with kindness, an old friend or place or version of the self offering a gift. Upright, this card honours genuine warmth toward what was — the childhood memory that still nourishes, the reunion that rekindles innocence, the kindness given freely as a child gives.
cards.reversed_meaning: Reversed, the same nostalgia either curdles into stuckness — idealising the past to avoid the present, refusing to grow up, the memory becoming a refuge from life — or begins to release its grip, the figure finally able to leave childhood behind with gratitude rather than grief. The shadow is not the remembering itself; it is the unwillingness to let the past be the past. The card invites you to ask which memory you have been living inside rather than learning from, and what forward step the tenderness is actually asking to free.
cards.psychological_lens: autobiographical memory + the role of positive nostalgia in identity; the difference between nourishing remembrance and stuckness in an idealised past
cards.eastern_anchor: The Six of Cups resonates with the East Asian image of '童心' (child-heart, the uncalculated sincerity of the child) and the practice of meeting one's younger self with kindness rather than judgement. Moonstone, traditionally a stone of inner-child work and the receptive memory of the heart, pairs with this tender turning backward.
cards.recommended_practice: Hold a moonstone at the heart; bring to mind one tender childhood memory that still nourishes you. Let it warm you for one breath — then name one way its gift is asking to be carried forward, not relived.
cards.love_meaning: In love readings, the Six of Cups signals tenderness, nostalgia, and often reunion — a reconnection with someone from the past, or a relationship whose warmth carries the innocence of an earlier time. Real sweetness; ensure it is meeting the present, not replacing it.
cards.career_meaning: In career, this card can point to returning to a beloved earlier path, a mentor or place from the past, or work that reconnects you to what you loved before the world complicated it.
cards.finances_meaning: For finances, the Six of Cups can signal a gift from the past — an inheritance, a returning favour, a resource remembered and reclaimed. Strategic framing, not specific investment advice.
cards.love_meaning_reversed: Reversed in love: stuck in an idealised past connection, refusing the present relationship for a memory, or nostalgia finally releasing toward something new.
cards.career_meaning_reversed: Reversed in career: refusing to grow past an early chapter, or — gift — finally leaving a nostalgic role with gratitude and moving forward.
cards.finances_meaning_reversed: Reversed in finances: clinging to a past financial arrangement, or releasing it toward a more current strategy. (Not investment advice.)

cards.name: Seven of Cups
cards.suit_theme: emotion / intuition / relationship / creativity of the heart
cards.progression_meaning: illusion / many options / the seduction of fantasy — Water's assessment node clouded, the shadowed figure facing seven cups each holding a different shimmering temptation
cards.progression_position: 7 = the assessment/choice node of Water's progression clouded by fantasy: where the Seven of Pentacles assesses a real harvest, Cups' Seven is seduced by possibility itself — seven cups float in a cloud, each holding a different tempting image (a wreath, a snake, a castle, jewels, a dragon, a shrouded figure, a laurel), the figure unsure which, if any, is real
cards.upright_meaning: Seven cups float in a mysterious cloud before a shadowed figure; each cup holds a different tempting image — a victory wreath, a serpent of wisdom, a castle of ambition, a hoard of jewels, a dragon of power, a shrouded mystery figure, a laurel of recognition. The Seven of Cups is Water's mirage: the moment possibility itself becomes more seductive than any single choice, fantasy multiplying options without testing any against the real. Upright, this card honours the genuine richness of imagination while gently observing that none of the cups has yet been touched to see which is real.
cards.reversed_meaning: Reversed, the same mirage begins to clear — clarity returning, the choice finally made, fantasy meeting reality and either dissolving or solidifying into something that can actually be lived. The shadow of the upright was untested possibility; the gift of the reversal is the willingness to choose and the discipline to test. This card invites you to ask which of the floating cups you have been admiring longest without touching, and what one small real-world test would reveal about whether it is a true offering or only a cloud.
cards.psychological_lens: fantasy proneness + the paradox of choice; the documented paralysis that arrives when possibility multiplies without commitment to test
cards.eastern_anchor: The Seven of Cups resonates with the Chan Buddhist observation that '镜花水月' (flowers in a mirror, the moon in water) are beautiful precisely because they cannot be grasped — and the suffering that comes from confusing the mirage with the real. Amethyst, traditionally a stone of sober seeing and the cooling of illusion, pairs with this card's call to test fantasy against reality.
cards.recommended_practice: Hold an amethyst at the third eye; name one floating cup you have been admiring longest. Identify one small real-world test this week that would reveal whether it is a true offering or only a cloud.
cards.love_meaning: In love readings, the Seven of Cups signals romantic fantasy or many options clouding a real choice — the imagined partner, the 'what if', the chemistry that has not been tested by daily reality. Discernment is the work.
cards.career_meaning: In career, this card points to many tempting paths and the risk of chasing possibility rather than committing to one — multiple opportunities that need testing against real conditions rather than admired in the cloud.
cards.finances_meaning: For finances, the Seven of Cups warns against fantasies of gain — the 'too good to be true' option, the speculative imagination. This is strategic framing about discernment, not specific investment advice.
cards.love_meaning_reversed: Reversed in love: clarity returning about a partner or a choice, fantasy meeting reality, the relationship tested rather than imagined.
cards.career_meaning_reversed: Reversed in career: the path chosen, options narrowing, the mirage dispersing as real conditions are engaged.
cards.finances_meaning_reversed: Reversed in finances: the speculative fantasy released, a real financial path tested and chosen. (Not investment advice.)

cards.name: Eight of Cups
cards.suit_theme: emotion / intuition / relationship / creativity of the heart
cards.progression_meaning: leaving / walking away / seeking deeper meaning — Water's movement node, the figure turning from eight stacked cups to climb toward higher ground because what was built no longer fills the heart
cards.progression_position: 8 = the mastery/movement node of Water's progression turned inward: where the Eight of Wands is outward velocity and Eight of Pentacles is outward mastery, Cups' Eight is the courage to leave — eight carefully stacked cups abandoned, the figure with a staff and a flask walking into the mountains toward a meaning the present arrangement no longer holds
reversed_keywords: stuck in what no longer fills
cards.upright_meaning: A figure with a staff in one hand and a small flask in the other walks away from eight cups carefully stacked on the ground; the moon above is in eclipse, and the path leads upward into rocky mountains. The Eight of Cups is Water's courage to leave: the moment the heart recognises that what has been built — however carefully stacked — no longer fills it, and the willingness to walk toward a meaning the present arrangement cannot hold. Upright, this card honours the difficult dignity of moving on, the pilgrimage toward deeper fulfilment, the inner call answered rather than muted.
cards.reversed_meaning: Reversed, the same call to leave is either refused out of fear — staying in what no longer fills, the cups still stacked but the heart emptied — or acted on prematurely, fleeing one situation before the real reason for leaving has been understood. The shadow is not the leaving itself; it is the confusion of avoidance with the pilgrimage. The card invites you to ask whether you are leaving because the situation has truly been outgrown, or because the inner work the situation is asking for has not yet been done.
cards.psychological_lens: intrinsic-goal re-orientation + the courage of self-concordant change; the difference between leaving toward meaning and fleeing from discomfort
cards.eastern_anchor: The Eight of Cups resonates with the East Asian image of '行脚僧' (the wandering monk who leaves the settled temple to seek the deeper teaching) and the Buddhist recognition that some attachments must be walked away from rather than renegotiated. Labradorite, traditionally a stone of transformation and the traveller between worlds, pairs with this pilgrimage away from what no longer serves.
cards.recommended_practice: Hold a labradorite at the heart; name one situation you have been considering leaving. Ask whether the leaving is toward a deeper meaning or away from work that has not yet been finished — and let the answer, not the mood, decide the timing.
cards.love_meaning: In love readings, the Eight of Cups signals walking away from a relationship that no longer fills the heart — or the inner recognition that the time to leave is approaching. The card asks whether the leaving is toward meaning or away from work not yet done.
cards.career_meaning: In career, this card points to leaving a role or path that has been carefully built but no longer carries meaning — the courage to seek deeper fulfilment, possibly at the cost of what has been stacked.
cards.finances_meaning: For finances, the Eight of Cups can signal walking away from an income source or arrangement that no longer aligns with deeper values. Strategic framing, not specific investment advice.
cards.love_meaning_reversed: Reversed in love: fear of leaving, staying in a relationship that no longer fills, or departing prematurely before the real reason has been understood.
cards.career_meaning_reversed: Reversed in career: stuck in what no longer serves, or fleeing before the inner work of the current role is complete.
cards.finances_meaning_reversed: Reversed in finances: clinging to an arrangement that has ceased to nourish, or leaving for the wrong reasons. (Not investment advice.)

cards.name: Nine of Cups
cards.suit_theme: emotion / intuition / relationship / creativity of the heart
cards.progression_meaning: satisfaction / emotional wish-fulfilment / contentment arrived — Water's near-completion node, the figure sitting satisfied before nine stacked cups with arms folded in the smile of arrival
cards.progression_position: 9 = the near-completion / satisfaction node of Water's progression: where Swords' Nine is the famous shadow, Cups' Nine is its mirror — the 'wish card', the figure seated before nine cups arranged in an arc behind him like a satisfied smile, arms folded, the body at ease with what has been received
reversed_keywords: the smile that hides a want
cards.upright_meaning: A figure sits on a bench, arms folded across a contented chest; behind him nine cups are arranged in a graceful arc like the curve of a satisfied smile — the picture of emotional arrival, the wish received, the body at ease with what has been given. The Nine of Cups is Water's wish fulfilled: where Swords' Nine sits up at 3 AM, Cups' Nine sits back with a smile. Upright, this card honours genuine emotional contentment — the moment a hope is met, a wish arrives, the heart says 'yes, this is enough' without performance or pretence.
cards.reversed_meaning: Reversed, the same contentment either wobbles — the wish fulfilled but somehow empty, smugness replacing satisfaction, overindulgence chasing the feeling that the original wish was not quite the right one — or simply delays, contentment approaching but not yet landed. The shadow is not the satisfaction itself; it is the discovery that getting what was imagined does not always deliver what was actually wanted. The card invites you to ask which wish you have been chasing that, now closer to fulfilled, is revealing itself as not quite the heart's true desire.
cards.psychological_lens: hedonic adaptation + the affective forecasting error; the documented gap between imagined and actual satisfaction, and the role of accurate desire
cards.eastern_anchor: The Nine of Cups resonates with the East Asian ideal of '知足常乐' (one who knows contentment is always joyful) and the recognition that true satisfaction depends less on the wish fulfilled than on the accuracy of the wish. Rose Quartz, the house stone of the heart across both Western and Eastern traditions, pairs with this emotional arrival.
cards.recommended_practice: Hold a rose-quartz at the heart; name one wish that has recently been closer to fulfilled. Ask whether the satisfaction matches the wanting — and if not, what the heart was actually asking for beneath the surface wish.
cards.love_meaning: In love readings, the Nine of Cups is one of the most contented omens — a relationship (or a moment within one) where the heart says 'yes, this is what I wanted'. Real emotional fulfilment, with the gentle caveat to check the wish was true.
cards.career_meaning: In career, this card signals genuine satisfaction in a role, a project completed well, recognition that matches effort, the moment of 'this is what I came for'.
cards.finances_meaning: For finances, the Nine of Cups can signal a financial wish closer to fulfilled — a goal met, a comfort arrived. Pair with the discernment of whether the having matches the wanting. Not specific investment advice.
cards.love_meaning_reversed: Reversed in love: the wish fulfilled but somehow empty, smugness replacing warmth, or the discovery that the imagined partner was not the actual one wanted.
cards.career_meaning_reversed: Reversed in career: the goal reached but hollow, or contentment delayed as the real wish clarifies itself.
cards.finances_meaning_reversed: Reversed in finances: the financial goal met but unsatisfying, or overindulgence chasing a feeling the original wish could not deliver. (Not investment advice.)

cards.name: Four of Swords
cards.progression_meaning: rest / restoration / deliberate stillness — Air's stabilization node, the knight lying in effigy beneath three swords hung on the wall and a fourth beneath him, the mind finally permitted to lie down
cards.progression_position: 4 = the stabilization/rest node of Air's progression: where the Two of Swords held a defensive truce, the Four of Swords is a chosen stillness — the knight lying in effigy on a tomb, hands folded in prayer, three swords hung on the wall above and one beneath the body, the mind taken offline for genuine restoration
cards.upright_meaning: A knight lies in effigy on a tomb, hands folded in prayer; three swords hang on the wall above him and a fourth rests horizontally beneath his body, while a stained-glass window of the Virgin and Child watches over the scene. The Four of Swords is Air's chosen rest: after the heartbreak of the Three and before any further action, the mind is permitted to lie down, to go offline, to recover. Upright, this card honours genuine recuperation — meditation, deliberate pause, sleep, the recovery that is not avoidance but the necessary stillness between one effort and the next.
cards.reversed_meaning: Reversed, the same rest is either resisted — restlessness, the mind refusing to lie down, burnout that will not admit its need — or simply complete, the body ready to rise again after a real pause. The shadow is not the activity itself; it is the refusal of the restoration the system is asking for, or its opposite, rest curdling into avoidance. The card invites you to ask whether you are resting because the body needs it or hiding because the next step feels costly, and to let the answer set the timing.
cards.psychological_lens: rest-and-digest recovery + the role of sleep and meditation in consolidating cognition; the difference between restorative rest and avoidant withdrawal
cards.eastern_anchor: The Four of Swords resonates with the Chan Buddhist practice of '止观' (śamatha-vipaśyanā, calm-abiding then clear-seeing) — the deliberate stillness that prepares the mind for clearer action, not a substitute for it. Amethyst, traditionally placed under the pillow or at the third eye for restful, sober sleep, pairs with this mind permitted to lie down.
cards.recommended_practice: Hold an amethyst at the third eye; name one area of your life where the mind has been running too long. Permit it one full night — or one full hour — of genuine offline rest.
cards.love_meaning: In love readings, the Four of Swords signals a relationship in a necessary pause — a breathing space, a time-out, the deliberate stillness that allows clarity to return before the next conversation. Rest, not withdrawal.
cards.career_meaning: In career, this card points to the need for genuine rest after sustained effort — sabbath, sabbatical, meditation, the recovery that prevents burnout. The pause is part of the work.
cards.finances_meaning: For finances, the Four of Swords can signal a pause in financial decision-making — a deliberate holding pattern while clarity returns. Strategic framing, not specific investment advice.
cards.love_meaning_reversed: Reversed in love: restlessness, a pause resisted, or — gift — the rest complete and the relationship ready to re-engage.
cards.career_meaning_reversed: Reversed in career: burnout refusing to rest, or the pause complete and readiness to return.
cards.finances_meaning_reversed: Reversed in finances: restlessness in financial decisions, or the pause complete and clarity restored for action. (Not investment advice.)

cards.name: Five of Swords
cards.progression_meaning: conflict / self-interest / winning at cost — Air's conflict node, the figure gathering swords from a defeated field while two figures walk away defeated and a third smirks at the costly win
cards.progression_position: 5 = the conflict/limit node of Air's progression: the rest of the Four has ended in rivalry — three swords gathered greedily by a smirking figure, two opponents walking away bent and defeated, the sky dark with the cost of a victory that has cost more than it gained
cards.upright_meaning: On a windswept shore, a smirking figure gathers three swords from the ground; two opponents walk away bent in defeat, and two more swords lie scattered behind. The Five of Swords is Air's hollow victory: the moment the mind has won — the argument, the deal, the case — but the cost has emptied the win of its meaning. Upright, this card does not curse; it names the genuine experience of conflict in which winning has come at the cost of relationship, integrity, or the larger field, and it asks whether the swords gathered are worth what was lost to gather them.
cards.reversed_meaning: Reversed, the same conflict begins to release — reconciliation, the cost finally recognised, amends becoming possible, the willingness to put down the gathered swords and re-enter the field differently. The shadow of the upright was self-interest dressed as victory; the gift of the reversal is the recognition that some wins are not worth having. This card invites you to ask which victory you have been pursuing whose cost has quietly exceeded its value, and what reconciliation the field is actually waiting for.
cards.psychological_lens: competitive self-interest + the pyrrhic victory; the documented gap between winning and wellbeing, and the role of perspective-taking in de-escalation
cards.eastern_anchor: The Five of Swords resonates with the East Asian virtue of '和为贵' (harmony is to be prized) and the martial wisdom that the battle won by destroying the field is a loss. Sodalite, traditionally a stone of honest, logical self-inquiry, pairs with this card's call to examine the real cost of the victory.
cards.recommended_practice: Hold a sodalite at the throat; name one 'win' you have been pursuing whose cost may exceed its value. Ask honestly whether the gathered swords are worth what was lost to gather them.
cards.love_meaning: In love readings, the Five of Swords signals conflict in which one or both partners are pursuing victory at the cost of the relationship — winning the argument, losing the bond. The card asks whether being right is worth what it is costing.
cards.career_meaning: In career, this card can signal rivalry, office politics, a deal or case won at the cost of relationships or integrity. Examine the real cost of the win.
cards.finances_meaning: For finances, the Five of Swords can warn against pursuing financial gain at the cost of integrity or relationship. This is strategic framing about the cost of self-interested tactics, not specific investment advice.
cards.love_meaning_reversed: Reversed in love: reconciliation, the cost recognised, amends becoming possible, the conflict released.
cards.career_meaning_reversed: Reversed in career: moving past conflict, recognition of the hollow victory, rebuilding the working relationship.
cards.finances_meaning_reversed: Reversed in finances: releasing self-interested tactics, recognising the cost, restoring integrity. (Not investment advice.)

cards.name: Six of Swords
cards.progression_meaning: transition / moving toward calmer water / the mind leaving turbulence behind — Air's harmony node, the boat moving across water from choppy shores to smoother ones
cards.progression_position: 6 = the harmony/passage node of Air's progression: the conflict of the Five has been left behind, and now a woman and child are ferried across water by a boatman, six swords standing upright in the vessel's bow — the deliberate journey from turbulence toward calmer understanding
cards.upright_meaning: A woman wrapped in a shawl and a small child sit in a flat-bottomed boat; a boatman poles them across calm water toward a distant shore, and six swords stand upright in the vessel's bow — the picture of a deliberate journey from turbulence toward peace. The Six of Swords is Air's passage: the moment the mind, after heartbreak and conflict, accepts the work of moving toward calmer understanding, often with the help of another who knows the waters. Upright, this card honours genuine transition — leaving choppy shores, ferrying the lessons (and the swords) forward, regaining composure at a pace the nervous system can sustain.
cards.reversed_meaning: Reversed, the same passage meets resistance — unwillingness to leave the turbulence, the boat stalled mid-crossing, the baggage that refuses to be ferried, calmer waters delayed by attachment to the conflict being left. The shadow is not the slowness itself; it is the refusal to fully board the boat. The card invites you to ask what you are still carrying from the choppy shore that the boat cannot hold, and what it would mean to let it be ferried rather than clutched.
cards.psychological_lens: post-stressor recovery + the role of guided transition; the documented benefit of moving, often with support, from dysregulated to regulated states
cards.eastern_anchor: The Six of Swords resonates with the East Asian image of '渡' (dù — to ferry across) and the bodhisattva who rows beings from the turbulent shore of suffering to the farther shore of peace. Aquamarine, traditionally the sailor's talisman for safe passage across water, pairs with this calm ferrying of the mind toward steadier ground.
cards.recommended_practice: Hold an aquamarine at the throat; name one turbulence you are currently being ferried away from. Speak one thing you are still clutching from that shore that the boat cannot hold, and let it be set down.
cards.love_meaning: In love readings, the Six of Swords signals a relationship transitioning toward calmer ground — often with help (counselling, a move, a new phase), leaving behind a turbulent chapter for steadier understanding. Real passage; honour the pace.
cards.career_meaning: In career, this card points to a transition out of a difficult role or workplace toward calmer, better-suited conditions — possibly with a guide or mentor who knows the waters.
cards.finances_meaning: For finances, the Six of Swords can signal a financial situation moving from turbulence toward stability through deliberate, supported transition. Strategic framing, not specific investment advice.

cards.career_meaning_reversed: Reversed in career: stuck in choppy conditions, the move delayed by attachment or fear.
cards.finances_meaning_reversed: Reversed in finances: the stabilising transition stalled, or baggage from the past preventing calmer waters. (Not investment advice.)

cards.name: Seven of Swords
cards.progression_meaning: strategy / stealth / acting from the edge — Air's assessment node turned indirect, the figure slipping away from the tent with five swords gathered and two left behind
cards.progression_position: 7 = the assessment/strategy node of Air's progression turned indirect: where the Seven of Pentacles assesses a harvest and Seven of Wands defends a position, Seven of Swords slips away — the figure crouching from a striped tent with five gathered swords, two left behind in the ground, a strategic withdrawal that may be wisdom, deception, or both
cards.upright_meaning: A figure in a red cap crouches as he slips away from a striped tent, five swords gathered in his arms and two left standing in the ground behind him; in the distance, the camp he is leaving. The Seven of Swords is Air's indirect strategy: the moment the mind chooses the edge over the direct confrontation, the hidden move over the declared one. Upright, this card does not curse; it names the genuine complexity of a moment that may be wise tactical retreat, necessary stealth, or self-serving deception — and it asks honestly which of these is operating.
cards.reversed_meaning: Reversed, the same indirectness begins to surface — disclosure, the hidden revealed, coming clean, the strategy abandoned for honesty, or the figure who slipped away choosing to return. The shadow of the upright was the hidden move; the gift of the reversal is the willingness to bring it into the light. This card invites you to ask which strategy you have been running in the shadows that is asking to be brought into honest conversation, and what realignment the situation needs.
cards.psychological_lens: indirect action + the ethics of concealment; the documented role of strategic thinking and the documented cost of deception when it hardens into a pattern
cards.eastern_anchor: The Seven of Swords resonates with the East Asian martial tradition of '以迂为直' (the indirect path can be the straightest) and the Buddhist ethical reminder that strategy untethered from honesty corrodes the one who employs it. Sodalite, traditionally a stone of honest self-inquiry, pairs with this card's call to examine which mode of indirectness is actually operating.
cards.recommended_practice: Hold a sodalite at the throat; name one strategy you have been running indirectly. Ask honestly whether it is wise tactical retreat or self-serving concealment — and what disclosure would realign.
cards.love_meaning: In love readings, the Seven of Swords signals hidden dynamics — withholding information, indirect communication, or one partner moving strategically around the other. The card asks for honesty about which indirect moves are wise and which corrode the bond.
cards.career_meaning: In career, this card can point to working behind the scenes, strategic non-confrontation, or — shadow — office politics and withholding. Discern which mode is operating.
cards.finances_meaning: For finances, the Seven of Swords can warn against hidden financial dealing or strategies that cannot withstand honest scrutiny. This is strategic framing about disclosure and integrity, not specific investment advice.

cards.career_meaning_reversed: Reversed in career: coming clean, abandoning counterproductive strategy, returning to declared action.
